misconceptions.counterexample: In a discrete metric space, any infinite set is bounded (diameter ≤ 1) but not compact: the cover by singletons has no finite subcover.
misconceptions.explanation: Compact implies bounded in ℝⁿ (Heine-Borel), but not in general metric spaces.

misconceptions.counterexample: ℝ is closed in itself but not compact: {(-n, n)} is an open cover with no finite subcover.
misconceptions.explanation: In ℝⁿ you need BOTH closed AND bounded (Heine-Borel). In general, you need the ambient space to be compact for a closed subset to be compact.

misconceptions.claim: every subset of a compact set is compact
misconceptions.explanation: Only CLOSED subsets of compact sets are compact. Open subsets can fail to be compact.
